sentences.text: Afternoon Richard, how are you?
Hello, Hunter, I'm good.
How are you doing?
I'm doing fantastic.
How's, uh, how's your week been?
You know what, it's been um a wild week.
I saw you had an emergency pop up.
I had a driver who was not at one of my residences, uh, or who was not one of my residents back up a car into one of my buildings.
So luckily no one is hurt, which is the most important thing.
Um, but yeah, so, you know, every day is something new.
I'm sure you guys work with lots of multi-family, uh, companies.
There's there's always something new.
Yeah, I think, uh.
Depending on how you look at it, it could either be a blessing or a curse, where every day you don't know what you're gonna get.
Yeah, exactly.
Where, uh, where are you based out of?
I'm in Silicon Valley, so I'm, uh, specifically in Santa Clara, um, right next to San Jose, about 30 miles from San Francisco.
Yeah, nice.
What about
yourself?
Uh, I'm in Scottsdale, Arizona.
Oh, Arizona,
OK, yeah.
Were you born or raised there?
No, I'm a Southern California boy.
I'm from LA.
Oh cool, yeah, yeah, well, I mean, it's very unfortunate what's going on there right now.
I'm going to get horrible, yeah, yeah, it's, it's horrible.
I've been seeing like so many videos and it's, I don't know, kind of mind blowing.
Yeah, no, it's really, really sad.
I've got so many friends and family down there and it's just constantly like checking on people like, you guys OK?
is everyone doing all right?
What's the status?
It's, it's very scary.
Yeah.
Um, well, I appreciate you, uh, being able to hop on the call, even with the, the emergency popping up.
No,
absolutely, thank you for being so flexible and for allowing me to push back a little bit.
Yeah, of course.
Um, so yeah, I'm definitely looking forward to the call today.
Uh, I want to have a back and forth conversation with you around market data, data, uh, processes that you all have in place.
I'll walk you through the platform and then uh at the end, we can decide if it makes sense to continue the conversation, um, and if not, that's totally OK.
How does that sound?
Is it just you and me today, Hunter?
I noticed a lot of people on this invite.
Oh yeah, it's just you and me, but my colleague Matt was gonna join but I think he got caught up in something, so he had to head out early for the day.
Got it.
OK, perfect.
Yeah, sounds good.
Perfect.
So I saw uh in the notes that I believe one of your colleagues had asked you to take a call with us, so kind of curious if you could deliver any insight into like a reason for that.

So, um, my, uh, new supervisor, she just got enrolled, I wanna say about a month ago, um, is Anna Dole, and she's now the VP of sales for Irvine Company to give you kind of a backstory, um, Irvine Company is a company where we have apartment communities, specifically coastal communities, um, so we're always near the water and we operate exclusively within California.
Um, we do have office buildings in New York and Chicago, but as far as residential, we're only in California.

She is located in Orange County, which is in Southern California, um, and I, of course, um, am up here, so there are 3 different directors that work underneath her in Orange County and then I work, um, I run Silicon Valley on my own and so, um, she, uh, I believe Ali reached out to her at some point, um, to learn more about apartment IQ.
And um I I think I'm the only person on our team that had that I've worked with other companies, not just Irvine Company um most of my peers have only ever been with Irvine Company.

I've worked with several other um large property management companies, so she asked me to um jump on the call and and learn more about it, um, and then I believe Ali also reached out to one of my peers here in Silicon Valley, Vera.
Um, and asked her, and so Vera had asked me if I had already had a meeting set up and I said yes.
So I think she responded back to Ali, but that's kind of the backstory.
So my role here is to learn more um from you and then to take that feedback back to Anna.
Sure, that sounds good.
Uh, curious, what all do you know about us as far as uh the company or the product, anything?
Nothing.
The only thing that I've heard about, um, is that I've heard some people comparing it to co-star, and I don't know how accurate that is, um, but that's really the only thing, um, I know.

I know that I will have many questions for you as far as how you are gathering this data, but, um, but I assume that you will, you will cover that, um, I'm sure you're aware there's, you know, a huge lawsuit in, um, you know, happening, and I, I think part of it was just settled with, uh, mainly Graystar but.

Um, you know, Congress got involved as far as sharing market information between companies and making it appear that we are setting rates, um, you know, sort of out pricing people and that being illegal, so, um, it's been very difficult to kind of share and gather marketing information if it's not publicly accessible on websites so I'm, I am curious how you're gathering that information and what there if there's any concerns on the way you distribute it, but I, I'm sure you'll go over that with me.
Yeah, I will definitely cover that.
Don't worry.
Um, that's definitely a hot topic.
It comes up on every single call, so
yeah,
we're all scared.
Yeah, I could definitely touch on that, um.
Which I'll definitely, uh, I'll I'll go over.
I'm, I'm curious as far as processes right now for collecting uh market data, who do you guys utilize as far as software or is it a manual process?
What does that look like?
It is a very manual process.
Um, this is the only company that I have ever worked for, uh, where we do not utilize a product like CoStar.
Um, I worked with, uh, every other company that I worked with, um, you know, gets a co-star report, um.
In my, are you familiar with Co-star?
OK.
In my experience, I've never been a fan of Co-star.
I think Co-star is a little outdated.
I don't think the information is very accurate.
I've also worked with um.
If you're familiar with the company Radix, yeah, and, um, Radix in my experience was not beneficial because it depended on people participating and in my experience, not a lot of people participated.
Um, so our market gathering information is very manual and again it's limited to only what we can access um on websites.
We are no longer able to call other buildings and say, hey, what's your occupancy right now?
What's your availability?
Um, we can only, uh, garner information that is available to the public.
Mhm.
So I'm assuming that, um, either I guess site teams are just going around and and scrubbing websites for all this data.
Correct.
OK.
How often do you think that that's getting done?
It's getting done region by region a little bit differently.
um, here we're doing it once a week.
um, I think we've got a pretty tight process of it up here, but my portfolio is much smaller than, say, for example, Orange County.
So my portfolio up here, I have a total of 11,000 units across 10 different buildings in Orange County you're looking at 66,000 units across 83 buildings.

So the regions are much, which is why there's 3 directors down there and I'm the only one up here, um, so it's, it's much different so I'm, I'm confident in the information that we're gathering up here but I'm not confident that that information is being gathered consistently um in every region, specifically down south.
Yeah, there's just
too many.
There's too many,
yeah, that's a lot.
Mhm, that's a lot to keep up with.

Um, OK, yeah, it's good to know, manual process, scrubbing the websites, um, and then let me ask you this, when it comes to pricing, uh, are you guys making pricing decisions like at the unit level, or you're doing it based off of like floor plans versus like or similar floor plans?
how typically like does that work?
So again it's gonna, it's gonna vary um region by region, um, it's gonna sound like I'm bragging to you, Hunter, um, because I think we have a pretty good grasp of it up here.

I have been given the grace to kind of set my own pricing, um, and so I set pri, I make pricing adjustments twice a week, um, you know, depending on the season that we're in or how we're doing, it will either I will either set pricing.
Um, by building, meaning, OK, we're gonna drop this building 1% across the board, we're gonna increase this building, um, 1.5% across the board.
If, um, for example, right now we're doing really well for this time of year.
Winter is typically a slow season in property management, um, and my availability is extremely low.
So I'm now doing pricing adjustments based on floor plans.

So raise this floor plan up 1%, decrease this floor plan, you know, 1.5%. In Orange County again and and I keep referencing Orange County just so you know because Orange County is where we're headquartered it is the bulk of our business, um, you know, and then we have kind of like satellite portfolios like myself sort of on the outskirts but um they have a pricing team which will make pricing adjustments.

Whenever they see fit, um, and the onsite teams are able to sort of make suggestions or recommendations, but, um, my opinion is that the team down there is, is much more removed from the pricing conversation and I am up here because my portfolio is so much more manageable.
OK, yeah, I think that makes sense.
Um, that definitely helps kind of get me.
The information I need so I can know what exactly to present to you today

um and I think it'll be important too as we continue this conversation and you know when you know um looping in for example Ana because each region is going to be very different, um, but the bulk of where you know I think your product from what I understand of it will be successful would be in Orange County.
Yeah, yeah, it's good to know.
Um, I think that's pretty much everything I need just of like right now as far as figuring out your processes.
So I'll give you kind of the rundown on us and like what we do, and then I'll share my screen and I'll walk you through it.
But uh apartment IQ is essentially designed to fully automate this process.
So uh what you're getting with us is a live data feed to where when you and your teams log in every single day, or to updates every 24 hours.
Everything that I'm gonna show you today is 100% public data.
So similar to how you guys,
yeah, no, it really is.
And what's funny is, um, we have been collecting 100% public data for the last 5 years.
And so because the lawsuit happened, it kind of played perfectly into how our business model worked.

And so I would say over the past year and a half we have transitioned over, uh, yeah, some of the largest names in the in uh in the industry over to apartment IQ off of, you know, co-star radix, uh Seric, whoever it is, you name it.
A lot of it with it being because of the lawsuits, but then a lot of it just because um how accurate and updated our information is every single day, saves a lot of time.
So 100% public data, uh, fully automated, we're tracking about 31 million units every single night, um.

And the cool thing about us that separates us from like ras or different providers is we can track anybody as long as they have a public uh an online website with online leasing, we can get you all the performance metrics that I'll show you today.
So they don't have to be an apartment IQ customer, um, all they need is a website with online leasing and we can track everything for you.
So the tool itself.
You got a question?

I, I was gonna, and you're probably gonna cover this, so forgive me, um, which companies are you currently working with as far as like that are utilizing your product, not necessarily the ones you're tracking, but who is actually participating?
Totally, uh, so we have the entire uh Willow Bridge Lincoln portfolio.
I just signed on the entire Avenue 51, same with Cerra Regis, uh, we work with Cortland, um, I think we're like 8 out of the top 10 as of right now.
Can I ask you who um did you work with anybody at Avenue 5?
Yeah, yeah, I was, I ran that whole deal.
Who did you work with over there?
Uh, Mellie.
Parks
Melanie, I don't think I know Melanie.
I know Latrice, um, she used to be an Irvine Company employee, and I think she just recently went to Avenue 5.
Yeah,
she, uh, she was on a handful of calls with us as well.
She's got, uh,

yeah, so Melanie, she's the, she's the CPO that was my main point of contact there, um, but I was working with the CEO as well and a handful of others in the C-suite, but um, yeah, they did a they did a full portfolio rollout, uh, I think Q3 of last year is when I signed them.
Oh, OK, cool, thank
you.
Yeah, of course.
Um, so the tool itself has about 4 different functionalities.
I'll briefly go over all of them and then I'll show you.

So one of them is going to be for the day to day operations where your teams log in, you're getting a live data feed on not only your assets, but every single comp that you're tracking, and the easiest way to explain it is where you can see how your units and floor plans are positioned within each specific concept that you're tracking.
One of them is for the day of the operations.
Um, another one is called Research, where you can spin up these custom market surveys for any sort of asset that you don't currently own or manage in a matter of 5 seconds.
All you have to do is type in the address.
The market survey will customize and spin up, and our system will pull what we believe to be the best 10 comps to that asset.
So it's great for maybe if you're looking to acquire property down the street, maybe you want some more insights into a market that you're currently not in.
That's a good functionality.
The Third one is going to be called a Research, which is going to be similar to Costar, where you can do a top down look into any sort of market, submarket, zip code, or neighborhood.
A lot of performance metrics there, a lot of historicals.
Um, we do give you 4 years worth of historicals in the app as well.
So I mentioned this earlier, I believe we're the only data provider in the space who's been tracking 100% public data for the last 5 years.
What criteria are you using to pick those 10 comps?
Great question.
So that is going to be based off of a few different metrics.
Give me one second.
I know geography will probably be like an obvious one, but you can have a, you know, a tier one building next to a tier 3 building too, which wouldn't necessarily be a com.
So, uh, what we're basing it off of is going to be distance to your property, you're built to renovated, total number of units in the asset, and we'll do some pricing and amenity matching.
Um, so that's how that gets graded.

The coll I'll show you when I share my screen too, but you can take a look at this in a couple different ways, but we'll actually give each asset a comp score based off of all those metrics, and it'll be listed from high to low.
Um, the other nice thing about our platform is you're in full control of your compet, so you can edit comps as often as you like, and that's going to be user specific.
So if you have a regional, uh, you can create permission to change comps as often as you like or sightseeing anything like that, fully customizable.
Um,
Let's see, continuing where I left off.

Third one is gonna be research, like I said, similar to Costar top down look into any sort of market submarket zip code neighborhood, and then the last one is going to be a pricing and recommendation.
So what we do is we analyze every single unit that you have, and we let you know if we think it's overpriced or underpriced, and we comp that against other similar units that are in that concept.
So we don't make pricing changes for you.
It's as simple as, you know, you know, unit 1234 is flagged.
It's a one bed, one bath, 750 square feet.
We think it's overpriced by $150 and then we'll list out all the units that are similar and so what they're priced at and to why we're giving that recommendation.
OK.
Any questions off that, otherwise I'll share my screen and walk you through it.
I'm a visual person, so I like being able to see everything.
No,
go for it.
Awesome.

guy.

yeah, so, uh, technically we are under the rentable umbrella, so that's our sister company.

we own ourselves, which is nice,
so relatively new.

So what we do is we actually move that to the applications bucket.
So as long as unit 1234 does not come back on the website within 7 days, we will then transfer that to a lease.
If it does come back on the website within 7 days, we'll count that as a cancel or denial.
So working with some of our larger clients that is how they prefer it, so you kind of have like a 7 day grace period, um, and we've just found that to be the most accurate.

sort of like deductive reasoning then, right?
Like you're if if it was on the website and it's not we're we're saying that it's leased.

OK, um, because I, so for me, for example, um, I have, uh, like some of my larger sites like my biggest site is 2700 units and so there's no way for me to get all of my available apartments on the website and so what I'll do is I'll have, um, it's not what I do, um, the way the system is set up is that there is an algorithm that takes like.
